story: Video of the March 2025 fatal shooting of VICTIM obtained by CBS News appears to contradict claims by federal officials that VICTIM was shot by an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT because VICTIM "accelerated" and "intentionally ran over" another LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT with VICTIM car. The killing of Martinez, who was 23 at the time, in South Padre Island, Texas, on March 15, 2025, was reported by local news outlets at the time. But it was not until February of this year, nearly 11 months later, that ICE confirmed one of its LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS had fatally shot VICTIM. In an internal report released by a nonprofit watchdog group last month, ICE said VICTIM "accelerated forward" and struck an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT during the March 2025 incident. The Department of Homeland Security claimed in a statement that an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT had fired "defensive shots" into VICTIM vehicle after VICTIM "intentionally ran over" another LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT. But body camera video, which has not been previously reported, shows that VICTIM vehicle, a blue Ford Fusion, was stationary or going at a very low rate of speed when VICTIM was fatally shot. When gunshots are heard in the video, the brake lights of VICTIM vehicle appear to be on. After VICTIM's shot three times, VICTIM is seen being pulled from VICTIM vehicle, thrown to the ground by an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT, face down, and then handcuffed. Personnel on the scene are not seen in the video providing medical care until after VICTIM is handcuffed. The Texas Department of Public Safety investigated VICTIM fatal shooting, though a grand jury last month declined to return criminal indictments in the case. "We stand by the grand jury's unanimous decision that found no criminality," acting ICE director Todd Lyons told CBS News in a statement Saturday. "This incident was investigated from every possible angle by an independent body, and it cleared our LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT." Lyons' statement went on: "According to the investigative report done by the Texas Rangers which included analysis of multiple body cameras, footage shows VICTIM 'holding a bottle of Crown Royal Whiskey' and 'rolling toward an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS location.' LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS yell 'where are VICTIM going' and 'stop VICTIM.' At this point, an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT directing traffic 'was directly in front of the Fusion' and 'only one-half a car length away.' VICTIM 'rolled forward and made an immediate left turn.' The LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT then 'appears to move as if he were on the vehicles hood.'" DHS' official version of events had already been previously called into question by somebody at the scene. Joshua Orta, VICTIM best friend and a passenger in the vehicle during the shooting, said in a draft declaration that VICTIM "did not hit anyone" and that VICTIM was trying to comply with commands from LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS. Orta died in a separate car crash last month, before he could sign that declaration. Texas DPS released dozens of records related to the investigation late Friday, including a video of an interview that members of the Texas Rangers conducted with Orta. During the police interview, Orta said he and VICTIM had a few drinks earlier in the evening, and were driving from Whataburger to a friend's condo when they encountered an area with heavy police presence. At one point, a police LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT told VICTIM to stop the car, and VICTIM seemed to get "jittery" and "panicked," according to Orta. He said that "out of reaction" VICTIM "kind of pushed the gas," but "VICTIM didn't floor it, it was barely moving." Orta said VICTIM turned the wheel left and the car moved slightly. An LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT "got on the hood a little bit" after his feet may have gotten caught, though he said he didn't think VICTIM hit the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT. After that, Orta heard an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT yell "stop" and then heard gunshots. Asked why VICTIM didn't stop the car, Orta said he thought VICTIM was "panicky" and "didn't know what to do." He suggested at one point that VICTIM may have been nervous about an open container in the car. Orta also said at another point during the interview he was concerned that VICTIM would get cited for driving while intoxicated. "VICTIM definitely didn't want to go to jail, but as far as running over an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT and endangering, VICTIM wouldn't do that," Orta told the two interrogators. In her first television interview since VICTIM death, Rachel Reyes told CBS News she has struggled to find "closure" because she had not received any videos or reports about VICTIM killing nearly a year after. She called on investigators to be transparent and for federal officials to reform how immigration LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS conduct their duties. "I don't blame President Trump for the death of VICTIM, 'cause he wasn't the one who pulled the trigger," Reyes said, after noting she voted for Mr. Trump in 2024. "But I do think that something needs to be changed in that department as far as the pattern of violence or abuse and impunity." What happened in the body camera video? The newly obtained video in question stems from a body camera worn by a South Padre Island ??police LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT. ICE has said the federal Homeland Security Investigations LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS involved in VICTIM shooting were helping local police LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS control traffic after a car accident. Nearly 21 minutes into the video, VICTIM vehicle is seen approaching an area with a heavy presence of local, state and federal law enforcement LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS. Someone can be heard saying "keep going." VICTIM car is seen moving forward. The vehicle stops for a group of pedestrians. At one point, some LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS appear to become concerned, with one yelling "stop VICTIM" several times, followed by "get VICTIM out." The LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS rush towards VICTIM vehicle, including the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT with a body camera. Moments later, three gunshots are heard. Before and during the moment those shots ring out, VICTIM vehicle appears to be moving very slowly, if at all, and the brake lights can be seen. The video showed the rear of VICTIM car when the shots were fired, so any activity near VICTIM was not visible when VICTIM was shot. After the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT shot VICTIM, the vehicle is seen moving slowly. One LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT is heard saying "stop the f—ing vehicle." Then, the car comes to a complete stop. The occupants of the vehicle, VICTIM and Orta, are directed to exit the vehicle. The video captures an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT removing VICTIM from the car and throwing VICTIM to the ground. Face down, VICTIM's later handcuffed. The LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS who restrained VICTIM are not seen providing medical care immediately after VICTIM's removed from the vehicle. The video also shows Orta being taken into custody. At around minute 23, emergency responders are seen rendering care and checking VICTIM body for exit wounds. VICTIM was shot three times roughly two minutes earlier. In a statement, Charles M. Stam and Alex Stamm, the lawyers representing Reyes, said the footage raises further questions about the official account of the March 2025 shooting. "These new videos confirm that VICTIM car was barely moving when VICTIM was shot. That VICTIM was braking, not accelerating. That nobody was on the hood of VICTIM car. That nobody was in front of VICTIM car when VICTIM was shot. That VICTIM was shot at point-blank range through VICTIM side window by an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT who was in no danger," Stam and Stamm said. The body camera also captured what appears to be an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT providing a preliminary briefing to the South Padre Island police chief at the scene about a half hour after the incident. In that exchange, the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT claims VICTIM "stepped on it" and was "on top of the other LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS in front" before being shot. He does not mention any LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS being injured. Orta said he and VICTIM went to South Padre Island to celebrate VICTIM birthday, saying they hung out with friends and had food and drinks the night of the shooting. A toxicology screen taken after VICTIM death detected alcohol and marijuana in VICTIM system. Asked about that screen in a recent interview, Stam, the family's lawyer, said VICTIM "was never stopped on suspicion of public intoxication or driving under the influence or anything of that nature." "It's important to bear in mind that when VICTIM lost VICTIM life, VICTIM car was in park, and right after those three bullets came through the window and went into VICTIM chest from that LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT, Mr. Orta's statement, said that VICTIM said, 'I'm sorry, sir,' as VICTIM last words," Stam added. "VICTIM was not someone who posed a threat." In the preliminary briefing caught on body camera footage, the South Padre Island LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT alleges VICTIM admitted to having alcohol in VICTIM vehicle.

story: A federal immigration LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT shot and killed VICTIM in Texas in March 2025, months before the Trump administration began its deportation surge in Minnesota that led to the shooting deaths of Renee Good and Alex Pretti, according to records released this week. VICTIM was killed by LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS with the Department of Homeland Security, attorneys for VICTIM family said in a statement. A DHS LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT fired multiple rounds at Martinez, who allegedly hit another DHS LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT with VICTIM car as the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS assisted local police in South Padre Island, Texas, with traffic control following an accident on March 15, 2025, according to records obtained by American Oversight, a nonprofit watchdog group. The LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS were conducting immigration enforcement, the records show. VICTIM shooting appears to be the first known instance of a US citizen being killed during US President Donald Trump's crackdown on immigration. LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS conducting immigration enforcement shot at least five people in January alone, including Pretti and Good. Family lawyers call for investigation VICTIM was trying to comply with instructions from local law enforcement when VICTIM was shot, Charles Stam and Alex Stamm, attorneys for VICTIM family, said in a statement. They called for a 'full and fair investigation.' 'VICTIM family has been pursuing transparency and accountability for nearly a year now and will continue to do so for as long as it takes,' Stam and Stamm said. The mounting death toll in Trump's intensifying immigration push has increased scrutiny on the crackdown, driving a backlash among lawmakers and the public. The Trump administration is dramatically ramping up immigration enforcement with $170 billion budgeted for immigration agencies through September 2029, a historic sum. VICTIM was taken to a hospital in nearby Brownsville, Texas, where VICTIM was later pronounced dead. The LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT who was hit with a car went to the hospital for a knee injury and was later released, according to the report. 'These records paint a deeply troubling picture of the violent methods used by ICE,' Chioma Chukwu, the executive director of American Oversight, said in a statement. 'In just the first months of this administration, ICE's own data shows a dramatic spike of nearly 400 percent in use-of-force incidents — with people hospitalized, bystanders swept up in operations, and even the death of a U.S. citizen.' A DHS spokesperson said in a statement that VICTIM 'intentionally ran over' an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT with DHS' Homeland Security Investigations and another LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT 'fired defensive shots.

story: A LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT shot at VICTIM in Black Forest, about 30 minutes north of Colorado Springs, Thursday morning. VICTIM allegedly tried to drive VICTIM vehicle into the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS during an enforcement operation. “As a result of VICTIM violent actions and to protect the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS and LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS onsite, an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT fired three shots into the vehicle,” an ICE spokesperson said in a statement. “VICTIM fled and later abandoned the vehicle. Law enforcement officials are currently searching for VICTIM.”  No injuries have been reported. Officials say VICTIM are still at large. A large law enforcement presence could be seen in the area Thursday. That included large armored vehicles, helicopters and drones, FBI LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS, LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS and El Paso County Sheriff’s deputies.  A KRCC reporter witnessed an abandoned truck towed out of a ditch by law enforcement, though it was unclear if that was the vehicle involved in the shooting. Burgess Road in Black Forest near the intersection of Green Tree had been closed for five hours, according to law enforcement. A vehicle gets towed near the scene of an incident involving LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS and suspects. Armed law enforcement LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS appeared to be searching a field and private properties near Burgess Road for VICTIM. By Thursday evening a shelter-in-place order that had been announced earlier in the day had been lifted, but it was unclear if any arrests had been made.  Pro-immigrant activists were at a law enforcement staging location by Thursday afternoon attempting to find information.  “We know that ICE is going to put up their own presentation on this, and the feds are going to put up their own presentation on this,” said Genevieve Richard with the Colorado Springs Alliance Against Racism and Political Repression. “We view anything they say with a great deal of skepticism, so we're just trying to find out all we can.” The ICE spokesperson said LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS are facing an increase in assaults and blamed “lies and violent rhetoric” for the increase.

story: On Thursday, EPSO received a request for assistance from federal partners with Homeland Security (HSI) and Immigration and Customs (ICE) after an incident where VICTIM tried to ram VICTIM car into LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS near an active construction site in the Black Forest area. “While Federal LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS were conducting targeted enforcement and investigation operations, they were met with violence and responded with the use of force, resulting in a critical incident,” EPSO said. Deputies responded to the call and set up a containment area of the crime scene to preserve its integrity and ensure a thorough investigation. Various resources, including deputies assigned to the Patrol Division, School Resource LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS, and the deployment of drones, were used. EPSO deputies also knocked door-to-door in the neighborhood to ensure community members were safe. The shelter-in-place has been lifted from the 9900 block of Burgess Road, according to an updated Peak Alert from the El Paso County Sheriff’s Office. U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) has confirmed that VICTIM tried to ram VICTIM car into LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS on Thursday, July 31, prompting a law enforcement response on Burgess Road in Black Forest. A Peak Alert was initially sent out shortly before 10:30 a.m. on Thursday morning, telling neighbors in a quarter-mile radius of the 9900 block of Burgess Road to shelter in place due to law enforcement activity. VICTIM fled and later abandoned the vehicle. Law enforcement officials are currently searching for VICTIM.”The Federal Bureau of Investigation Denver (FBI Denver) released the following statement to FOX21 on Thursday. “FBI Denver is investigating an alleged assault on a LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT that occurred in El Paso County, Colo. earlier today. As this is an ongoing investigation, we do not have any further information to provide at this time.”ICE said there were no injuries reported as of Thursday afternoon, and a heavy law enforcement presence is still active at the Burgess Road location. The public is urged to avoid the area.

story: LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS opened fire during a targeted immigration enforcement operation in San Bernadino, California, on Saturday, calling it an act of self-defense after a man “struck two LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS with his vehicle,” the Department of Homeland Security said. But the family inside the vehicle said they drove away out of fear for their safety after masked men emerged from unmarked cars and surrounded their truck with weapons drawn before smashing the truck’s windows. Martin, one of the three men in the vehicle, told CNN affiliate KABC he was working with his father-in-law and 18-year-old brother-in-law on Saturday morning when their vehicle was suddenly surrounded. The LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS did not identify themselves and the family stayed inside the locked car, said Martin, who KABC only identified by his first name. Videos recorded from inside the family’s truck show at least three masked LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS wearing tactical vests marked with “police” surrounding the car. At least one of the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS is wearing a hat marked “CBP.” The LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS are seen asking the men inside the truck to roll down the window, but the men refuse. The LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS are then seen breaking two windows in the truck before the driver accelerates away. It’s unclear why DHS was pursuing the men in the vehicle. “Once the glasses were shattered, my father-in-law, fearing for his life from his perspective … he drove the vehicle away from where those LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS were at,” Martin told KABC. It is unclear in the videos when LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS were struck by the vehicle. In its statement, DHS said two LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS were injured. It did not specify the severity of the injuries. As the truck drives away, three apparent gunshots can be heard in the videos and one video shows what seems to be bullet holes in the side of the truck. “Thank God the bullets didn’t go through the truck. They just went in, got stuck into the door, but they were aimed at my brother-in-law, who was in the passenger front,” Martin told KABC. Once home, the men called San Bernardino police to report the incident, Martin said. At 9:30 a.m., about an hour after the vehicle stop, armed immigration and Homeland Security LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS, along with San Bernardino police, surrounded the family’s home, according to the Inland Coalition for Immigrant Justice. They remained at the scene until 3:45 p.m., despite never presenting a warrant, “pressuring the individual to come outside and placing the entire family in fear,” the organization said in a statement to CNN. “This is a clear abuse of power. Firing at civilians, detaining families without cause, and silencing community voices is unacceptable and must end,” the organization said. DHS said local police “located the subject at a residence and briefly had him in custody,” but “he was set free” and LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS left the scene. “This decision was made despite the subject refusing to comply and wounding two LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS — another terrible example of California’s pro-sanctuary policies in action that shield criminals instead of protecting communities,” DHS said in a statement. According to KABC, San Bernardino police cited the California Values Act, which prevents local law enforcement from helping federal officials with immigration enforcement actions. Police told KABC they later returned to the scene after a request from federal officials due to a large crowd forming as LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS attempted to arrest a man. The department said it learned the man, whose identity was not revealed, was wanted for assaulting a LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT.

story: VICTIM has been detained, VICTIM family tells FOX 11. What we know: Back on August 16, VICTIM and multiple passengers were inside a work truck in San Bernardino when a group of masked men tried to get them to get out of the vehicle. At one point during the standoff between VICTIM, the truck passengers, and the masked men who were later revealed to be LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS, one of the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS began smashing the work truck. The sudden attack prompted the driver of the truck to drive off from the scene. As the work truck took off from the scene, one of the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS opened fire at VICTIM and the passengers, prompting an ICE-involved shooting investigation. Videos showing parts of the incident went viral on social media. An organization called Inland Coalition for Immigrant Justice shared a post showing a driver and at least two passengers refusing to get out of a car and a LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT smashing the car's window, prompting the driver to take off from the scene. Fast-forward to more than a week later, VICTIM family revealed that VICTIM has since been taken into custody. According to VICTIM attorney, Homeland Security Investigations arrested VICTIM at VICTIM home around 4:18 a.m. Thursday, Aug. 28. VICTIM was arrested on a single charge of alleged Assault of a Federal LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT with a Deadly/Dangerous Weapon. VICTIM was arraigned later in the afternoon. VICTIM attorney said no arrest warrant was issued at the time of the August 16 incident and the government claimed ICE and LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS decided to perform an "immigration check" on VICTIM. The criminal complaint alleged that the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS involved in the stop had been injured by VICTIM truck. The court asked AUSA Cory Burleson to identify any injuries allegedly sustained by the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS. Burleson then acknowledged "I’m not aware of any injuries", according to a statement issued by VICTIM attorney.

story: A LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT fatally shot a man in the Chicago area who drove his car into LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS, a representative for the agency said on Friday, adding that the man had been resisting arrest during a vehicle stop. The man, who the authorities said was not legally in the country, dragged the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT as he fled in his vehicle, the agency representative said. The LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT was severely injured and was in stable condition. ICE identified the man as Silverio Villegas-Gonzalez, 38, who is from Mexico. He was pronounced dead at a local hospital. The LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT 'followed his training, used appropriate force and properly enforced the law,' Tricia McLaughlin, the assistant secretary for public affairs at the Department of Homeland Security, said in a statement. She added that 'viral social media videos and activists encouraging illegal aliens to resist law enforcement' were undermining public safety. The agency did not immediately respond to requests about whether the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS involved in the operation were wearing body cameras. ICE has been conducting a higher number of immigration arrests than usual in the Chicago area this week, under an effort called Operation Midway Blitz. That operation has led to the arrests of several people, including those accused of robbery, assault and other violent crimes, the agency said on Wednesday. Gov. JB Pritzker of Illinois, a Democrat who has criticized the federal immigration crackdown, said in a statement that he was concerned about the shooting. 'The people of Illinois deserve a full, factual accounting of what’s happened today to ensure transparency and accountability,' he said. In a news conference on Friday evening near the site of the shooting, elected officials and local activists also called for accountability from federal officials. 'We know the increasingly aggressive tactics of ICE do not keep our communities safe,' said Lawrence Benito, the executive director of the Illinois Coalition for Immigrant and Refugee Rights. Residents of Chicago and its suburbs have been watchful for LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS and vehicles, reporting activity to hotlines and websites that track the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS. Local officials said they had seen an uptick in sightings since last weekend. The shooting on Friday took place in Franklin Park, a suburb of Chicago near O’Hare International Airport with a significant Latino population. A stretch of the street where the shooting took place was closed off with yellow tape on Friday afternoon, and police LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS and municipal workers directed traffic away from the area. Shortly after 3 p.m., an F.B.I. evidence recovery team had left the scene, and the driver’s car was being towed. Jennifer López, 28, who lives in the neighborhood, said she was driving home on Friday morning when she was turned away by a police LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT. Ms. López said she did not see the shooting, but pulled over and saw what appeared to be LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS trying to perform lifesaving measures on a man on the ground. Since immigration arrests began in the area, many people in her neighborhood, regardless of their legal status, have been on edge, Ms. López said. 'They know when and where to go,' she said of immigration LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS. 'They’re not going downtown. They know where the Hispanic community is.' The Franklin Park Police Department referred questions about the shooting to the F.B.I., which said in an email that it was 'aware of the incident in Franklin Park and is assisting law enforcement in response.' The agency added, 'There is no threat to public safety or further information available.' Family members of Mr. Villegas-Gonzalez could not be reached. The fatal shooting comes after two other immigrants died in recent months while trying to avoid arrest during a similar blitz by LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS in Southern California. One Mexican farmworker fell from a greenhouse, and a Guatemalan day laborer was struck by a vehicle on a freeway when he ran from a raid at a Home Depot. In August, LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS opened fire on a truck that they claimed tried to hit them when its driver sped away after LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS broke its window. No one in the truck was injured. LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS have also been injured. In June, an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT was wounded while detaining a Mexican man in a suburb of Minneapolis. According to prosecutors, the immigrant refused to lower his window during a traffic stop, which prompted an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT to shatter it and reach inside the vehicle. The driver sped off, according to a criminal complaint, dragging the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT 100 yards, which resulted in gashes that required 33 stitches.

story: JB Pritzker is calling for more ""transparency"" after an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT was seriously injured during an encounter with VICTIM outside Chicago. VICTIM was shot and killed after allegedly dragging the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT for a significant distance with VICTIM car. ICE Deputy Director Madison Sheahan pushed back on Pritzker’s comments, accusing Democrats of siding with illegal migrants over law enforcement. ""They obviously continue to take the sides of these criminal illegal aliens who put our people in harm's way,"" said Sheahan Tuesday on ""Fox & Friends."" ""Not just LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS, it's the people of Chicago, people of these communities that these Democrats and Governor Pritzker continue to choose to protect."" People stand near a memorial with candles and flowers. The Department of Homeland Security identified VICTIM as Silverio Villegas-Gonzalez and said VICTIM resisted arrest and attempted to flee during a traffic stop. Sheahan said VICTIM, had a record of reckless driving and a final order of removal. When LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS tried to detain VICTIM, VICTIM allegedly drove toward them, striking and dragging one LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT. The LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT then opened fire. VICTIM was taken to a hospital where VICTIM later died. Sheahan said the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT was put ""in critical condition,"" but has since returned home. On Monday, Pritzker said he has requested more details about how the failed arrest unfolded, but has received few details. ""We need more information. We've asked ICE for all of the information around it. They have given very little,"" he said. ""ICE is unwilling to provide the transparency that I think the American public and the public here deserves."" Sheahan rejected the governor’s scrutiny. ""It is under investigation and we're [going to] continue to make sure we are transparent. The claims that we're not is just false,"" she said. The dispute comes as ICE expands ""Operation Midway Blitz"" across Chicago and Illinois. The effort is aimed at arresting illegal immigrants with criminal histories. However, Pritzker and Chicago Mayor Brandon Johnson have strongly resisted the operation. Earlier this month, Johnson wrote on X: ""Chicago doesn't want to see reckless, unconstitutional, militarized immigration enforcement in our city."" Sheahan has noted that anti-ICE rhetoric makes LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS’ jobs more dangerous. ""We're already at over a thousand percent increase year over year of the dangers that our LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS are in because of the rhetoric that we're seeing online, the dangers of what elected officials continue to say,"" she said. Despite the pushback, Sheahan said ICE will continue its mission with the backing from the White House. ""Law enforcement LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS overwhelmingly want to work with ICE to keep their [communities] safe,"" she said.

story: CHICAGO (WLS) -- VICTIM was labeled a "domestic terrorist" by the Trump administration last fall after VICTIM was shot five times by a U.S. LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT. But newly released evidence reviewed by the ABC7 Chicago I-Team paints a different picture of what actually happened the day VICTIM was accused of ramming LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS with VICTIM vehicle before one of them opened fire. While all criminal charges have been dismissed against VICTIM, the I-Team is learning more about an open criminal investigation into the shooting, and the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT who fired those five shots. At a Wednesday news conference, VICTIM, alongside VICTIM attorneys, announced VICTIM will soon be filing a civil lawsuit against the Department of Homeland Security. "Most people after their criminal indictment has been dismissed, would stop fighting," attorney Christopher Parente said. "But to have your own government call you something as hurtful and as harmful as a 'domestic terrorist' when you know you're not is just unacceptable." Contradicting Evidence It was Oct. 4 near 39th Street and Kedzie Avenue on the Southwest Side when footage from a LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT's body-camera starts recording, after VICTIM had followed the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS for several blocks, honking VICTIM horn. While the footage has been referenced in court, the public is now able to see it for the first time. U.S. Border Patrol LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT Charles Exum Jr. can be seen driving an SUV, along with three other LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS as passengers; all of the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS' faces were blurred by the government. "Do something, (expletive)," an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT is heard saying, while another LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT is seen pointing his handgun toward the window. "Alright it's time to get aggressive and get the (expletive) out because they're trying to box us in," an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT says as several trailing vehicles can be heard honking. "We're going to make contact." Surveillance footage reviewed by the I-Team shows VICTIM was on the driver's side of the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS' vehicle when LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT Exum can be seen swerving the SUV's steering wheel in VICTIM direction. He then stops the car, steps out and fires five shots. Exum's body camera wasn't recording at the time of the shooting; he initiated it after the fact. And minutes after, Exum tells LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS the use-of-force incident was his first. "I got out (of the vehicle) so we could defend," Exum says. Then, multiple LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS can be heard warning him to stay quiet. "Hey, hey, since we're recording, keep it... Keep everything out, you're good man," one LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT tells him. "You don't give statements to anybody," another LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT can be heard telling Exum. "Absolutely no statements at all, no statements, you keep your mouth shut." VICTIM attorney Parente says Exum's version of what led up to and during the shooting is contradicted by the evidence that was released after U.S. District Judge Georgia Alexakis ordered it unsealed last week. Judge Alexakis said the evidence has "impeachment value" on LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT Exum's version of events. "You can see the bullet went back to front, which is not only inconsistent with (LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT Exum's) statement to the FBI that all five shots went through the windshield," Parente said, "but it's also extremely troubling that he's shooting at a fleeing vehicle, which is a violation of the Border Patrol's published use of force policy." Parente argues the evidence contradicts initial Department of Homeland Security (DHS) reports that VICTIM rammed VICTIM vehicle into LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS and they were "boxed in." In surveillance video reviewed by the I-Team after shots were fired, VICTIM vehicle continues forward unimpeded, and no cars were blocking the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS' vehicle from the front. "The U.S. Attorney's Office recognized they can never call this guy as a witness in trial because the jury wouldn't believe a word he said," Parente said. A spokesperson for the U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) told the I-Team on Wednesday, "CBP is committed to the highest standards of conduct, transparency, and accountability. All significant use-of-force incidents are thoroughly investigated, reviewed, and presented to the National Use of Force Review Board (NUFRB)." "Consistent with policy, LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT Exum was placed on administrative leave," the statement reads. The agency did not answer questions about when LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT Exum's leave began, or whether it continues to this day. Ongoing Investigation With the public getting its first look at evidence in the case on Wednesday, one thing that's still locked in federal custody is VICTIM car VICTIM was driving the day of the shooting. That's because more than four months later, the federal government still possesses VICTIM vehicle due to an "ongoing and pending criminal investigation" into the shooting by the U.S. Attorney's Office in South Bend, Indiana, according to court testimony and filings reviewed by the I-Team, as well as VICTIM attorneys. The criminal case against VICTIM was dismissed with prejudice last November, meaning it can't be brought back again, and VICTIM attorney says the investigation is focused on LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT Exum. "This is what the prosecutors saw and realized that they had real problems with their witness, which is why this case was referred to the U.S. Attorney's Office in South Bend, Indiana, for a criminal investigation of LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT Exum," Parente said. A spokesperson for the Northern District of Indiana U.S. Attorney's office declined to comment Wednesday, citing a "pending investigation." According to DHS reports reviewed by the I-Team, Exum has been an immigration LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT since 2005. Exum testified in court that he is stationed in Maine but was on a temporary duty assignment to "Operation Midway Blitz" when the Oct. 4 shooting occurred. Before the criminal charges against VICTIM were dismissed by prosecutors, LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT Exum was accused of destruction of evidence when, after the shooting, he drove his vehicle out of state, and it was repaired by a mechanic before it could be inspected by VICTIM lawyers. Exum's vehicle was considered a key piece of evidence given the government accused VICTIM of ramming LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS with VICTIM vehicle, while VICTIM said it was the other way around and LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS had collided with VICTIM. The evidence tampering allegation led Judge Alexakis to summon Exum to court on Nov. 5, 2025, for questioning about what he did with his CBP vehicle, and to nail down his timeline of events. Exum testified that immediately after the collision and shooting on Oct 4, his government-issued Chevy Tahoe had intermittent scratches and dents on the driver's side, and some black marks on the driver's door and above the fuel tank door. Exum said the car was removed from the scene of the shooting and taken to an FBI office for evidence processing. Several hours after the shooting, Exum testified that he was told his vehicle was ready to be picked up from the FBI's Chicago office. He testified that he was told by his supervisor that the vehicle had been processed for evidence and that he was free to retrieve it. Over the course of three days, Exum testified that he drove the vehicle back to Maine, arriving at his home duty station and parking it in the CBP facility's garage on Oct. 10. "You can tell them that I said that (name redacted) could start to work on the vehicle," Exum's U.S. Border Patrol deputy LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT in charge writes. "If they needed (the vehicle) as evidence I did not think they would have released it from the investigation, had you drive three days all the way back to Maine, possibly destroying some of that evidence along the way if they still needed it." On cross-examination at the November hearing, Parente disputed Exum's version of events, citing FBI reports stating it was not a supervisor, but Exum who initiated the request for the vehicle repair. At Wednesday's news conference, Parente vowed to thoroughly investigate what happened, and to clear VICTIM name. "They picked the wrong victim in this case to mess with," Parente said. "VICTIM spent every day as a Montessori school teacher, not wherever people who are actual domestic terrorists spend their days.
text: Evidence appears to contradict the DHS account of a Chicago shooting. CHICAGO (WLS) -- VICTIM was labeled a "domestic terrorist" by the Trump administration last fall after VICTIM was shot five times by a U.S. LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT. But newly released evidence reviewed by the ABC7 Chicago I-Team paints a different picture of what actually happened the day VICTIM was accused of ramming LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS with VICTIM vehicle before one of them opened fire. While all criminal charges have been dismissed against VICTIM, the I-Team is learning more about an open criminal investigation into the shooting, and the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT who fired those five shots. At a Wednesday news conference, VICTIM, alongside VICTIM attorneys, announced VICTIM will soon be filing a civil lawsuit against the Department of Homeland Security. "Most people after their criminal indictment has been dismissed, would stop fighting," attorney Christopher Parente said. "But to have your own government call you something as hurtful and as harmful as a 'domestic terrorist' when you know you're not is just unacceptable." Contradicting Evidence It was Oct. 4 near 39th Street and Kedzie Avenue on the Southwest Side when footage from a LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT's body-camera starts recording, after VICTIM had followed the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS for several blocks, honking VICTIM horn. While the footage has been referenced in court, the public is now able to see it for the first time. U.S. Border Patrol LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT Charles Exum Jr. can be seen driving an SUV, along with three other LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS as passengers; all of the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS' faces were blurred by the government. "Do something, (expletive)," an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT is heard saying, while another LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT is seen pointing his handgun toward the window. "Alright it's time to get aggressive and get the (expletive) out because they're trying to box us in," an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT says as several trailing vehicles can be heard honking. "We're going to make contact." Surveillance footage reviewed by the I-Team shows VICTIM was on the driver's side of the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS' vehicle when LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT Exum can be seen swerving the SUV's steering wheel in VICTIM direction. He then stops the car, steps out and fires five shots. Exum's body camera wasn't recording at the time of the shooting; he initiated it after the fact. And minutes after, Exum tells LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS the use-of-force incident was his first. "I got out (of the vehicle) so we could defend," Exum says. Then, multiple LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS can be heard warning him to stay quiet. "Hey, hey, since we're recording, keep it... Keep everything out, you're good man," one LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT tells him. "You don't give statements to anybody," another LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT can be heard telling Exum. "Absolutely no statements at all, no statements, you keep your mouth shut." VICTIM attorney Parente says Exum's version of what led up to and during the shooting is contradicted by the evidence that was released after U.S. District Judge Georgia Alexakis ordered it unsealed last week. story: A judge just ordered a LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT back to Chicago after learning he drove a key piece of evidence more than 1,000 miles away. The Brief A LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT accused of shooting VICTIM drove the government SUV from Chicago to Maine after the incident. A federal judge ordered the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT back to Chicago, citing concerns over evidence preservation. New body camera footage reportedly shows LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS ramming VICTIM, contradicting the federal version of events. CHICAGO - Explosive new information came out in federal court in Chicago on Thursday. What we know: The LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT accused of shooting Marimar Martinez, who was hit five times, drove the government-issued SUV involved in the incident all the way from Chicago to Maine, raising fresh concerns about potential evidence mishandling. That’s over 1,100 miles — crossing multiple jurisdictions — after the shooting, after the crash, and after body-worn camera footage was recorded. The government acknowledged the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT’s assignment in Chicago had ended, and the vehicle was driven back to his home base in Maine. But the vehicle remains key to the investigation. According to court statements, VICTIM legal team wants access to inspect the SUV, but it’s now hundreds of miles away. Meanwhile, their vehicles are still being held as evidence. The judge overseeing the case responded sharply. Directing her comments to government attorneys, she ordered: "I am directing you to tell the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT to be brought back to Chicago as soon as possible," said Georgia Alexakis, a judge on the United States District Court for the Northern District of Illinois. The judge also noted that she’s worked in a professional manner with some of the attorneys in the case, but that wouldn’t stop her ability to be impartial. New footage contradicts federal narrative VICTIM attorney, Christopher Parente, revealed in court that body camera video shows LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS ramming into VICTIM — not the other way around. This directly contradicts LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS’ claims that VICTIM was a threat who rammed them. The footage also reportedly shows one LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT, moments before opening fire, aiming an assault rifle and shouting: "Do something, b---h." Call for preservation of evidence Parente is now filing an amended preservation order demanding that: All texts and communications from the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT who fired be preserved. Any repairs, inspections, or even car washes performed on the SUV be documented and preserved. All ICE and Homeland Security communications, including emails, texts, notes, and internal documents, be retained. This follows earlier inconsistencies uncovered by the media, including FOX 32's Oct. 7 report, showing discrepancies between what federal officials told the media and what the evidence shows. Where was VICTIM really? Homeland Security initially told the media that the incident occurred near Broadview, but court records now show VICTIM was in Brighton Park, warning residents about ICE patrols. Behind VICTIM was another vehicle driven by Anthony Ian Santos Ruiz, also following LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS. Officials claimed LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS were "boxed in by 10 cars" and one driver had a semi-automatic weapon. But police scanner audio and witness testimony contradict that narrative: VICTIM gun remained in VICTIM purse, according to police audio. VICTIM holds a valid concealed carry license. VICTIM was not charged for having the weapon. Photos and damage Photos submitted to the court show: VICTIM dark SUV hit on the passenger side. Ruiz’s SUV damaged in front. The federal vehicle, a silver SUV, with only minor damage. VICTIM was shot five times.

story: WASHINGTON (AP) — A LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT fired shots at VICTIM during a recent traffic stop while patrolling the nation’s capital for President Donald Trump’s law-enforcement surge. But a police report on the encounter doesn’t mention the shooting, an omission that VICTIM attorneys point to as evidence of a cover-up attempt. The Metropolitan Police Department is investigating the shooting by a Homeland Security Investigations LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT, who was with police LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS and other LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS when they stopped a car driven by VICTIM on Oct. 17. VICTIM wasn’t injured in the shooting. VICTIM was jailed for three days on a charge that VICTIM fled from law enforcement, but a judge has already dismissed the case. VICTIM lawyers claim the police department tried to cover up the shooting by leaving it out of the police report and refusing to provide them with video from police body cameras. At a court hearing for VICTIM criminal case, a police LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT testified that he was instructed not to include the shooting in the police report, according to civil rights attorneys Bernadette Armand and E. Paige White. They said police also failed to disclose the shooting to a prosecutor assigned to the case. The judge who dismissed the case against VICTIM ruled that there was insufficient evidence that VICTIM was fleeing, according to VICTIM attorneys. The gunshots struck VICTIM driver-side window and front passenger seat at chest level, the lawyers said. “We are lucky that VICTIM is alive. VICTIM could very well be dead,” White said. The LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT’s police report says VICTIM revved VICTIM sport utility vehicle’s engine and began driving toward law-enforcement LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS before VICTIM rear-ended another vehicle. A Department of Homeland Security spokesperson says the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT who fired his gun feared for his life “and the lives of others” when he fired “defensive shots” into the vehicle. “This incident is not isolated and reflects a growing and dangerous trend of vehicles being used as weapons against DHS law enforcement,” the DHS spokesperson said in a statement. “Our LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS are facing a 1000% increase in assaults against them including vehicle rammings, terrorist attacks, and even bounties for their murders. The violence must end.” Armand said it is “outrageous” for DHS to claim that the shooting was justified when there is no mention of the shooting in the police report. “Of course they’re going to say it was justified. What are they going to say? ‘We shot at VICTIMcar in a routine traffic stop for nothing?’ They’re not going to say that. They’re going to say whatever they have to say to justify their actions,” Armand said. A police report that doesn’t mention the shooting was filed in D.C. Superior Court for VICTIM criminal case. Metropolitan Police Department spokesman Tom Lynch provided The Associated Press with a copy of a separate report for the internal affairs division’s parallel investigation of the shooting. “We are the agency investigating the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT-involved shooting, and we have been continuously since Oct. 17th,” Lynch said. He declined to comment on the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT’s testimony about the omission of the shooting from the report on VICTIM arrest. In August, Trump, a Republican, issued an executive order declaring a crime emergency in Washington. For nearly three months, the White House has deployed hundreds of LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS and over 2,000 National Guard members to help police patrol the city’s neighborhoods. LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS from the FBI, U.S. Customs and Border Patrol, the Diplomatic Security Service and the U.S. Marshals Service also were patrolling with two MPD LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS and the HSI LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT who shot at VICTIM, according to the police report. They stopped VICTIM for having heavily tinted windows and no front plate on VICTIM sport utility vehicle, the report says. VICTIM attorneys say VICTIM traffic stop demonstrates the risky nature of patrols by LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS who aren’t adequately trained for police work. “It’s not OK to have LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS and LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS on the streets who are engaged in shooting at unarmed people and then covering it up after the fact,” Armand said. “There’s no trust there. There’s no accountability there. And there’s no credibility there.”A magistrate judge in D.C. Superior Court ordered VICTIM release on Oct. 21. VICTIM was traumatized by VICTIM arrest and experiences in jail, VICTIM lawyers said. They’re weighing a possible lawsuit over VICTIM arrest.

story: The Brief Lawyers for VICTIM, are accusing police of a cover-up and demanding bodycam footage be released. The shooting occurred October 17 on Benning Road after VICTIM was pulled over; an HSI LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT fired three shots but missed, and charges against VICTIM were later dropped. MPD denies a cover-up, saying an internal investigation began immediately, while activists urge Mayor Bowser and Councilmember Brooke Pinto to take action. WASHINGTON - Lawyers for VICTIM are demanding answers and transparency from police and the mayor’s office — also claiming there was a cover-up of the shooting. What we know: Lawyers and several community activists on Tuesday called on Mayor Muriel Bowser to end MPD’s working relationship with LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS — and to stop its cooperation with the Trump administration. The case they’re highlighting happened 11 days ago in the 3900 block of Benning Road. On the night of October 17, VICTIM was pulled over by D.C. police — working with LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS — for having heavily tinted windows. During that traffic stop, police say VICTIM car rolled toward LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS. MPD says an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT with HSI — Homeland Security Investigations — fired at least three shots at the car, missing VICTIM. Photos show two of the bullets hit the front passenger seat — which was empty. Criminal charges against VICTIM were later dropped. Now, VICTIM lawyers say they want any body-worn camera footage released — and for the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT who fired the shots to face charges. They’re also accusing MPD of a cover-up, claiming the initial police report makes no mention of shots being fired. "We have no evidence or information whatsoever about who these LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS are — and if they’re still patrolling the streets. If they’re still on the force. If they still have a weapon. And so this is a very serious community issue that needs to be addressed now," said attorney E. Paige White.  "You can’t be part of a ‘Make America Safe Again’ initiative when you’re shooting at unarmed people in a routine traffic stop. The feds aren’t making D.C. streets safer — they’re making them more dangerous," said attorney Bernadette Armand.  The other side: Bowser’s office today declined to comment. D.C. Police, however, say there was no cover-up of the shooting — and that an Internal Affairs investigation began the same night as the incident. MPD says because the shooting did not involve one of its own LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS, the department is not required to release any body-worn camera footage without a court order.

story: LOS ANGELES (KABC) -- An investigation is ongoing after VICTIM was shot during an immigration operation in South Los Angeles on Tuesday morning. A deputy U.S. marshal was also injured by a ricochet bullet. The shooting happened in the 400 block of East 20th Street near Trinity Street around 8:52 a.m. Federal officials said it began as a targeted enforcement traffic stop on VICTIM. A view from AIR7 over the scene showed a dark-colored sedan boxed in by several other vehicles. Witnesses told Eyewitness News VICTIM is a well-known TikToker who regularly follows ICE. VICTIM goes by Richard LA online. Witnesses said VICTIM is a well-known TikToker who regularly follows ICE. VICTIM goes by Richard LA online. DHS said VICTIM's an undocumented immigrant. Acting U.S. Attorney Bill Essayli identified VICTIM as 44-year-old Carlitos Ricardo Parias. VICTIM's charged with assault on a LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT and is expected to appear in court on Wednesday. If convicted, VICTIM faces up to eight years in federal prison. In Spanish, Pier Rojas, who also takes video of LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS, told Eyewitness News that VICTIM was in VICTIM car, and it appeared VICTIM was nervous and afraid, not wanting to get out of the car. The witness said LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS then fired rubber bullets and tear gas. In a statement, Tricia McLaughlin, assistant secretary for the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), said VICTIM "weaponized VICTIM vehicle and began ramming the law enforcement vehicle in an attempt to flee. Fearing for the safety of the public and law enforcement, our LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS followed their training and fired defensive shots." VICTIM is accused of ramming VICTIM Toyota Camry into law enforcement vehicles in front of and behind VICTIM after they boxed VICTIM in. Essayli said VICTIM spun the Camry's tires, spewing smoke and debris into the air and causing the car to fishtail, "causing LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS to worry for their safety." Essayli said an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT broke into VICTIM side window, but it wasn't enough to subdue VICTIM. That's when an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT opened fire. VICTIM was shot once in the elbow, and the deputy U.S. marshal was shot in the hand by a ricochet bullet, according to DHS. Essayli said both the deputy marshal and VICTIM are expected to recover. Friends say VICTIM is undergoing surgery on the gunshot wound. Crowds formed outside the California Hospital in downtown L.A., where VICTIM and the deputy marshal were taken for treatment. Back at the scene of the shooting, activists and community members gathered for a rally and press conference to protest ICE and denounce the arrest. "This level of violence from LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS is unacceptable," said Veritas Topete with Centro CSO. "This type of violence is a type of violence that we denounce, and is a type of violence that will only create more violence," said Ron Gochez with Union Del Barrio. "Vehicles are deadly weapons. Anyone who uses them against LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS risks arrest, imprisonment, and life-threatening injuries. We will continue to use every tool in our legal arsenal to protect our LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS and enforce immigration laws passed by Congress," Essayli said.

story: VICTIM was shot by immigration LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS while they tried to arrest VICTIM back in October. The Brief A federal judge dismissed the indictment against VICTIM, citing government failures to release body camera footage and a violation of VICTIM right to counsel. VICTIM was shot in the elbow by a LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT during an October immigration enforcement stop in South Los Angeles that also resulted in a deputy U.S. Marshal being wounded by ricochet. The original charges alleged assault on LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS after VICTIM reportedly rammed law enforcement vehicles with VICTIM car while being boxed in by authorities. LOS ANGELES - A federal judge dismissed the indictment against VICTIM, citing government failures to release body camera footage and a violation of VICTIM right to counsel. What we know: U.S. District Judge Fernando M. Olguin issued the dismissal after finding that VICTIM was deprived of access to VICTIM attorney while being detained in South Los Angeles. The judge also highlighted the government’s repeated failure to meet court deadlines, specifically regarding the release of body camera footage of the October shooting.  PREVIOUS COVERAGE: TikToker Richard LA, U.S. Marshal shot by LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT in South Los Angeles "In short, the government’s failure to coordinate the overlapping actions of its separate agencies while relentlessly pursuing VICTIM criminal proceedings created a situation from which constitutional violations could — and did — occur," Olguin wrote. The backstory: VICTIM was originally indicted for assault on a LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT and depredation of government property following an October incident in South Los Angeles where LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS shot VICTIM in the elbow after VICTIM allegedly rammed their vehicles to evade arrest on an immigration warrant. VICTIM was released on bond pending VICTIM trial and taken to an immigration detention facility in Adelanto. Despite the dismissal of the criminal case, U.S. authorities continue to hold VICTIM in administrative detention at the GEO Group-run facility, citing VICTIM status as a Mexican citizen living in the country illegally. Dig deeper: VICTIM is well-known for documenting ICE operations and had previously received a certificate of recognition from the Los Angeles City Council for VICTIM community reporting. VICTIM posts Spanish-language videos on two accounts with a combined 340,000 followers. Videos on VICTIM page dating back to 2024 document police activity, car accidents and fires, as well as tortilla-making and rainy days in Los Angeles. This year, the footage has also focused on demonstrations against immigration enforcement and arrests by federal deportation authorities. Timeline: June 13, 2025: VICTIM has a documented encounter with ICE while filming an arrest; VICTIM is briefly detained and released. August 2025: The office of Councilmember Curren Price awards VICTIM a Certificate of Recognition for VICTIM "citizen reporting." October 21, 2025: LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS surveilling VICTIM home box in VICTIM vehicle during a traffic stop. Gunfire erupts, wounding both VICTIM and a deputy U.S. Marshal. October 22, 2025: VICTIM is charged with assault on a LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT while hospitalized. December 29, 2025: A federal judge dismisses the indictment against VICTIM due to civil rights violations and procedural failures by the government. What they're saying: The case has sparked significant debate between federal authorities and community advocates: Attorney Carlos Jurado described VICTIM as a "pacifist" and argued that VICTIM was targeted for VICTIM social media coverage, stating, "What we know about VICTIM is that VICTIM's a very calm man... it's difficult to trust every word that comes from DHS." Acting U.S. Attorney Bill Essayli previously stated, "VICTIM... refused to comply with LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS' orders and escalated what could have been a simple arrest into a life-threatening situation." The Office of Councilmember Curren Price called VICTIM a "pillar of our community" and a "fearless citizen journalist" whose storytelling "uplifted the unheard voices of South Central Los Angeles." What's next: The U.S. Attorney’s office in Los Angeles said in a statement that the agency is evaluating its options for appeal. "We strongly disagree with the court’s version of the facts as well as its legal conclusions," the statement said. VICTIM remains in ICE detention in Adelanto.

story: VICTIM was one of several people wearing camouflage clothing spotted by LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS on the riverbank along the U.S.-Mexico border, west of the Rio Grande City Port of Entry, CBP officials said. As the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS approached just after 4 p.m., the group scattered, with some running back toward Mexico. LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS tried to apprehend VICTIMengaged in an “active struggle” for about two minutes, and ultimately the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT opened fire at least three times. Other LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS arrived at the scene, requested emergency medical help and performed CPR, the DHS said. VICTIM was pronounced dead at a hospital, CBP officials said.

story: VICTIM was left fighting for VICTIM life after being shot in an incident involving the US Border Patrol, the Pima County Sheriff's Department said. The early-morning shooting is now under investigation, with few details released about what sparked it. The incident happened in southern Pima County near milepost 15 of West Arivaca Road, about 45 minutes south of Tucson, at around 7:30 am on Tuesday. VICTIM has not been identified. Santa Rita Fire District said crews rushed in and transported VICTIM in critical condition. The department said Santa Rita Fire District and American Medical Response provided medical care at the scene. 'Patient care was transferred to a local medical helicopter for rapid transport to a regional trauma center,' the fire department said. Authorities have not said what led up to the shooting. It's also unclear whether any law enforcement LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS were hurt. The Pima County Sheriff's Department confirmed the shooting involved Border Patrol in a post to X. The shooting comes three days after Border Patrol fatally shot Alex Pretti in Minneapolis, and weeks after an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT shot and killed Renee Good. Earlier in January, Arizona Attorney General Kris Mayes, in an interview with 12News, warned that Arizona's 'Stand Your Ground' law, which lets people use deadly force if they believe they're in immediate danger, is a potential recipe for disaster if protesters come face-to-face with immigration LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS. 'It's kind of a recipe for disaster because you have these masked LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS with very little identification, sometimes no identification, wearing plain clothes and masks,' Mayes said. She added that ICE is 'very poorly trained.' 'And we have a Stand Your Ground law that says that if you reasonably believe that your life is in danger and you're in your house or your car or on your property, that you can defend yourself with lethal force.' Federal immigration LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS have shot 12 people since September as DHS has ramped up deportation operations around the country. The recent killings by immigration LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS have led to widespread protests across the country, with Minnesota being the epicenter. Protesters have also targeted the building they believed was housing immigration LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS. Demonstrators swarmed the property within 24 hours of the fatal shooting of Pretti, turning the scene into a flashpoint overnight. Protesters blocked off parts of the street with trash cans and other objects as they chanted anti-ICE slogans. The demonstration escalated fast, windows were smashed and the hotel exterior was tagged with 'ICE OUT,' footage showed. As the crowd pushed toward the entrance, people inside dragged two large vending machines to barricade the doors. The clash unfolded Sunday evening, drawing police and additional reinforcements as tensions rose. LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS later deployed tear gas and chemical irritants to disperse the crowd outside the hotel. The unrest came as President Donald Trump said he would send border czar Tom Homan to Minnesota to take over the response to the crisis. CNN then reported that Border Patrol official Gregory Bovino and several LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS were expected to depart Minneapolis on Tuesday.

story:
The chaotic apprehension of VICTIM in St. Paul, Minnesota, ended violently Sunday after VICTIM allegedly struck two LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS with VICTIM vehicle during a stop – causing one LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT to fire “defensive shots” – before VICTIM was ultimately apprehended, according to the Department of Homeland Security.

The arrest comes as the Trump administration continues its immigration enforcement efforts across the Twin Cities, which have stoked controversy in recent weeks over Immigration and Customs Enforcement tactics and concerns over racial profiling.

But Sunday’s pursuit points to a troubling trend that’s emerged during the administration’s immigration enforcement efforts, which left some officials warning about the potential for escalation and violence.

It began when LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS tried to detain VICTIM during a stop.

“VICTIM was noncompliant and refused to roll down VICTIM window, causing LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS to warn that they would have to break the window if VICTIM continued to not comply with lawful orders,” the agency said in a statement posted online.

That’s when VICTIM drove off, DHS said, and a chaotic chase began.

As VICTIM fled the scene, VICTIM allegedly struck one of the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS with VICTIM vehicle. VICTIM then drove into a nearby parking lot and hit two parked cars, according to DHS.

As LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS commanded VICTIM to exit the vehicle, VICTIM allegedly “began ramming VICTIM car into an ICE vehicle and struck another LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT,” DHS said in a statement.

“The LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT who was struck by VICTIM car defensively fired two rounds from his service weapon, causing VICTIM to drive off again,” the agency said. “No one was struck by any of the defensive shots fired.”

VICTIM, however, continued to try to elude apprehension, according to the agency. VICTIM allegedly rammed another ICE vehicle before attempting to flee into VICTIM apartment on foot.

The agency said LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS brought VICTIM to the ground as VICTIM “continued to violently resist arrest and bit one of the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS.”

The LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS involved sustained non-life-threatening injuries during the pursuit and were taken to a nearby hospital for evaluation, according to DHS.

VICTIM was also evaluated at a hospital and is currently in ICE custody pending further charges, the agency said.

It was not immediately clear if VICTIM had obtained legal representation.

CNN has reached out to ICE for further comment on the arrest.

In a statement posted on social media, Saint Paul Police said they arrived at the scene after the incident and learned VICTIM had been struck and fired VICTIM service weapon.

“No Saint Paul police LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS were involved in the arrest or use of force,” the department said.

Police use of force has become a lightning rod in Minnesota following the murder of George Floyd. In the aftermath, local police have spent years trying to reform department protocols during police encounters.

Minneapolis Police Chief Brian O’Hara alluded to how the federal police presence has at times complicated those efforts during a news conference Tuesday.

“We have been training our LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS for the last five years very, very intensely on de-escalation,” O’Hara said.

LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS chased VICTIM down and brought VICTIM to the ground, where VICTIM violently resisted arrest and bit one of the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS, ICE said. 

VICTIM was eventually subdued and placed in handcuffs. 

Two LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS sustained non-life-threatening injuries and were taken to a hospital for evaluation. 

VICTIM was also transported to a hospital for evaluation. 

ICE said VICTIM was admitted into the U.S. in 2024 under the Biden administration’s CBP One app. 

In April, migrants who entered the U.S. under CBP One had their protections terminated and were ordered to leave the U.S. immediately or face a permanent ban from reentering the U.S. 

The app was used by nearly 1 million migrants to schedule appointments at official ports of entry before they were paroled into the U.S. The migrants were permitted to seek asylum and given temporary work authorization for two years while they waited for the outcomes of their respective proceedings. 

Trump ended the use of the CBP One app to parole migrants on his first day in office. His administration has also paused applications for parole programs and allowed ICE to cancel parole statuses of migrants. 

The CBP One app launched in January 2023 and was used to admit more than 936,500 people through December 2024, the New York Post reported, citing DHS data. 

The Biden administration had expanded the use of the CBP One app to allow migrants to enter the U.S. at ports of entry or via a separate parole process. That process involved them uploading information including a photograph.

story:
Family and friends of VICTIM are urging local officials to arrest and prosecute the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT.

VICTIM, was fatally shot by an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT on New Year’s Eve outside VICTIM apartment complex, according to LA and federal officials. An LA police department (LAPD) spokesperson said after the incident that VICTIM had fired gunshots into the air. A US Department of Homeland Security (DHS) spokesperson said the off-duty immigration LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT was “forced to defensively use his weapon” while responding to an “active shooter”.

Much about the incident remains unclear. There’s no footage of the shooting. VICTIM family and local activists have argued that, contrary to the DHS’s portrayal of the events, VICTIM was not threatening anyone and was celebrating the new year. Jamal Tooson, an LA attorney representing VICTIM family, said VICTIM actions possibly merited arrest or citation by the LAPD, but the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT, who was not charged with local law enforcement duties, instead subjected VICTIM to “a death sentence”.

Activists, local politicians and some celebrities have rallied around VICTIM killing, with many of them voicing increasing concerns about ICE’s aggressive raids and violent tactics in cities across the country. And the DHS’s track record of making false and distorted claims about people they have accused of crimes and violence has fueled concerns that the government cannot be trusted to provide a truthful account of VICTIM death.

Those fears have only intensified in the wake of an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT’s fatal shooting of Renee Nicole Good in Minneapolis last week. Good’s killing sparked nationwide protests, with the DHS calling her a “domestic terrorist” without evidence and Donald Trump insisting the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT, Jonathan Ross, fired in self-defense as Good “ran him over” with her car. Footage, however, showed her car was turning away from the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT when he shot her, and that he did not lose his footing or appear to be injured during the encounter.

Organizers against police brutality, including Black Lives Matter Los Angeles, mobilized community members to testify at an LA police commission meeting on Tuesday, demanding the LAPD disclose the name of the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT who shot VICTIM and pursue criminal charges.

“I’m heartbroken and I know that ache will never go away,” Wanda Turner, a longtime friend of VICTIM family, who considered herself to be a second mother to VICTIM, said in an interview. “The LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT needs to surrender and do what’s right. I want him charged. Even if he thought VICTIM was doing something wrong, it wasn’t his place to just shoot and kill VICTIM.”

Late Friday afternoon, after publication of this story, Stacie Halpern, a criminal defense lawyer, confirmed she was representing the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT who shot VICTIM, and identified him as Brian Palacios.

She said Palacios’ shooting of VICTIM was lawful and in self-defense.

The shooting of VICTIMeral authorities have disclosed very few details about the ongoing investigation or what they believe happened between the two men.

The LAPD said in a statement that its LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS responded to 911 calls to the complex in LA’s Northridge neighborhood at about 10.40pm and found VICTIM “on the ground”, and VICTIM was soon after declared dead. The LAPD said the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT had “confronted” VICTIM, but declined to answer further questions about the incident.

ICE said in a statement that its LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT lived at the complex and had “exchanged gunfire” with VICTIM. “Fortunately, our brave LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT was not injured while protecting his community,” the statement said.

Tooson, the family lawyer, has said VICTIM may have fired “celebratory” gunshots to ring in the new year, telling the LA Times he believed several people were firing guns into the air. He also said a witness said they didn’t hear the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT identify himself as an LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT.

Tooson told reporters he did not believe there was “any exchange of gunfire” between VICTIM and the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT and suggested there was a “lack of corroborating witnesses and evidence, such as shell casings indicating VICTIM fired toward the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT”.

The outrage around VICTIM killing has swelled in recent days, with protesters holding signs with VICTIM and Good’s faces, saying “Murdered by ICE”, and celebrities at the Golden Globe awards on Sunday wearing “ICE Out” and “Be Good” pins to honor them.

In LA, longtime community activists and VICTIM family have organized several vigils and protests. At the LA police commission’s weekly meeting on Tuesday, speakers one by one condemned VICTIM killing and called on LA leaders to take swift action.

“When are we going to press charges on this murderer? When is he going to be named? When is he going to be arrested?” said Jsané Tyler, VICTIM cousin. She questioned whether the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT had been given a sobriety test or forced to turn over his service weapon and whether he took any steps to render aid. In Good’s shooting, witness videos showed LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS blocking bystanders who attempted to provide medical aid.

“This is systemic. When is there going to be value placed on our lives?” continued Tyler, who is part of Black Lives Matter LA. “As an organizer, I never thought I would be standing here for VICTIM … I urge you guys, regardless of federal intervention, to really help us, help VICTIM family, help our community get justice.”

Dr Melina Abdullah, a co-founder of Black Lives Matter LA who has been supporting VICTIM family, said that in addition to seeking the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT’s arrest and prosecution, “We’re demanding an end to the character assassination of VICTIM. VICTIM’s the victim, not the suspect … It’s disgusting the way DHS is talking about VICTIM.”

Halpern, Palacios’ attorney, said by phone on Friday that her client was “defending himself and the community” and repeated the assertions shared by ICE that VICTIM had fired at the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT. Palacios’ name was first reported by the LA Times.

LAPD declined to comment on his identification.

US law enforcement agencies have long faced backlash for demonizing and spreading misinformation about people killed by LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS. LA activists argued the DHS was particularly unreliable, pointing to its account of Good’s killing, which Minneapolis’s mayor called “bullshit” and the state’s governor said was “propaganda”. The DHS has also come under scrutiny for claiming LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS have been widely subjected to “assaults”. Court records reviewed by the LA Times last year revealed many cases of alleged attacks involved no injury to the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT, and prosecutions have repeatedly ended in dismissals and acquittals.

“We cannot trust what is being shared with us by entities representing LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENTS, because they have not been truthful,” said Eunisses Hernandez, an LA city council member, who has been supporting VICTIM family on Monday. “When protests happened last year … the federal government said Los Angeles was burning to the ground. That was not happening.”

Hernandez called for more transparency: “We need to know who these people are in our neighborhoods taking people’s lives. This LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT could still be participating in raids happening in my district.”

The DHS did not respond to detailed questions about the case and advocates’ criticisms and on Friday did not immediately respond to inquiries about the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT’s identity.

Greg Risling, a spokesperson for the LA district attorney, said in an email on Thursday his office responded to the scene and was investigating the case, and prosecutors would “review all evidence disclosed by the investigation to determine whether or not the law enforcement LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT acted lawfully”. On Friday, he declined to comment on the LAW_ENFORCEMENT_AGENT’s identity.

Hernandez brought VICTIM mother, Franceola Armstrong, to a city council meeting last week, where Armstrong recalled how VICTIM had called her every morning to say “VICTIM love you”. “VICTIM was a wonderful soul. VICTIM heart was big,” the mother said.

Turner, the longtime friend of VICTIM family, said it was painful to see officials “sully VICTIM reputation” and that VICTIM should be remembered for VICTIM joyful attitude: “VICTIM loved to make you smile.”

Adrian Metoyer III, a Los Angeles film-maker, said he and VICTIM had been best friends since they met as teenagers in 1996, bonding over sports: “VICTIM was hilarious, a joker, the life of the party.” Metoyer, now 45, served as a foster parent to teenage boys when he was in his 20s and recalled VICTIM support: “VICTIM was my second in command, my go-to guy to assist me in caretaking. VICTIM was always there to help people.”

VICTIM more recently held multiple jobs, at one point serving as an aide to students with special needs while employed at Home Depot, Metoyer said.

And VICTIM was a proud “girl dad”, Metoyer added; he shared footage of an interview he filmed with VICTIM in 2022 reflecting on VICTIM life. “VICTIM got two beautiful young girls and VICTIM’m going to raise them the way VICTIM’m supposed to,” VICTIM says in the footage while wearing a Dodgers shirt. “VICTIM pray. VICTIM talk to God. VICTIM talk to family. Because at the end of the day, that’s all VICTIM really got. But VICTIM feel rich … It’s gonna be hard to break VICTIM, because VICTIM have a strong spirit and VICTIM grew up with a whole lot of love.
